coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots for ultrasensitive detection of anticancer drug gefitinib based on IFE
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: He, Yuxin
Wang, Hanting
Yan, Yuting
Jiang, Xinhui
Zou, Hecun
Zhang, Zhengwei
coredata.dc:description: Gefitinib, a highly significant antitumor drug, is now commonly employed in clinical settings as a first-line treatment for patients with advanced or metastatic non-small cell lung cancer, colon cancer, and breast cancer. Herein, a convenient, rapid, and accurate fluorescence method based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) was designed for ultrasensitive detection of gefitinib. The NCDs were easily synthesized through one-pot hydrothermal process using p-phenylenediamine and D-glutamic acid as the precursors. The sensing strategy relied on the fluorescence of NCDs at 345 nm, which was selectively reduced by gefitinib based on the inner filter effect (IFE). With a broad linear range of 0.025–30 μg/mL and a low limit of detection of 5.5 ng/mL, the probe was successfully applied to the detection of gefitinib in human serum samples, demonstrating strong practicality, affordability, and high accuracy. The proposed sensor is simple in design, fast in detection and cost-effective, and exhibits promising application in drug real-time analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Gefitinib
Fluorescence sensing
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of gefitinib in real samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 82003713 and 82003854 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing ( cstc2020jcyj-msxmX0158 and cstc2021jcyj-msxmX0302 ), and CQMU Program for Youth Innovation in Future Medicine ( W0152 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image and the size distribution histogram of NCDs. (B) UV–vis absorption and FL spectra of NCDs. (C) FL emission spectra of NCDs excited with various wavelengths of 220–300 nm. (D) FT–IR spectrum of NCDs.
XPS survey (A) and high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (B), N 1s (C), and O 1s (D) of NCDs.
(A) FL spectra of NCDs after adding various concentrations of gefitinib (0–30 µg/mL). (B) Correlation curves between log(F/F0) and gefitinib concentrations (0.025–30 µg/mL).
The (F0-F/F0) of NCDs in the presence of gefitinib and various possible interfering substances (metal ions, amino acids, and other some drugs). Error bar represents the standard deviation (n = 3).

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of NCDs, gefitinib, NCDs-gefitinib, and superposition data of NCDs and gefitinib. (B) UV–vis absorbance spectrum of gefitinib, and FL excitation and emission spectra of NCDs. (C) Fluorescence lifetime decay curves of NCDs in the absence and presence of gefitinib. (D) FT–IR spectra of gefitinib and NCDs-gefitinib.
Schematic diagram of the synthetic process of NCDs and their application for detecting gefitinib based on IFE.
Comparison of analytical characteristics of various methods for gefitinib detection.
Recovery and precision tests of the analyses of gefitinib in real samples.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots for ultrasensitive detection of anticancer drug gefitinib based on IFE

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmacy, Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing 400016, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmacy, Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing 400016, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Forensic Medicine, School of Basic Medical Sciences, Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing 400016, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Life Sciences, Chongqing Medical University, Chongqing 400016, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Gefitinib, a highly significant antitumor drug, is now commonly employed in clinical settings as a first-line treatment for patients with advanced or metastatic non-small cell lung cancer, colon cancer, and breast cancer. Herein, a convenient, rapid, and accurate fluorescence method based on nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) was designed for ultrasensitive detection of gefitinib. The NCDs were easily synthesized through one-pot hydrothermal process using p-phenylenediamine and D-glutamic acid as the precursors. The sensing strategy relied on the fluorescence of NCDs at 345 nm, which was selectively reduced by gefitinib based on the inner filter effect (IFE). With a broad linear range of 0.025–30 μg/mL and a low limit of detection of 5.5 ng/mL, the probe was successfully applied to the detection of gefitinib in human serum samples, demonstrating strong practicality, affordability, and high accuracy. The proposed sensor is simple in design, fast in detection and cost-effective, and exhibits promising application in drug real-time analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Gefitinib is an orally active EGFR TKI (Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitor) widely used in cancer therapy [1]. It suppresses the intracellular phosphorylation of various tyrosine kinases related to transmembrane cell surface receptors, including EGFR-TKI [2–4]. Gefitinib has become a common first-line treatment in clinical settings for patients with advanced or metastatic breast cancer, colon cancer, and non-small cell lung cancer (NSCL), earning the distinction of being the first FDA-approved targeted agent for treating EGFR-mutant NSCL [5–7]. However, oral anticancer drugs often exhibit considerable interpatient variability in pharmacokinetics, resulting in significant variations in drug exposure [8]. Previous research has established a link between gefitinib exposure and the severity of adverse drug reactions, affecting the quality of life of the patient [9]. Common adverse effects include diarrhea, rash, dry skin, acne, vomiting, and nausea [10,11]. However, it has also been shown that approximately 11–26 % of patients receiving standard doses of gefitinib fail to attain target therapeutic concentrations in their bodies [8]. Hence, therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) is crucial for optimizing individual treatment, ensuring therapeutic efficacy, and preventing treatment resistance or toxicity [12]. In this context, it is very necessary to monitor the plasma concentration of gefitinib through effective analytical techniques.

Numerous analytical techniques, including surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [13,14], RP–HPLC [15,16], LC–MS/MS [17,18], and fluorescent probes [19], have been established for the quantitative detection of gefitinib. However, most of these techniques struggle with the issues of costly equipment, tedious operations and time-consuming pre-treatment, thus limiting their practical applications. Nanomaterials-based fluorescence sensors hold great promise compared to other technologies due to their high sensitivity, excellent selectivity, and rapid response. Among fluorescent materials, carbon dots (CDs) stand out for their ease of synthesis, surface modification capabilities, outstanding photostability, adjustable emission wavelength, excellent biocompatibility, and high fluorescence quantum yield [20–23]. The interaction between CDs and the analyte leads to changes in the fluorescent properties of CDs, including intensity, wavelength, and lifetime, resulting in multiple signals for sensitive analyte detection [24–26]. By the process of doping and introducing heteroatoms (like N, B, P, Br, and S), the efficiency of the physicochemical properties of doped CDs has enhanced significantly compared to CDs alone [27]. Carbon and nitrogen are the same size, ensuring a good interaction in nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs). The few valence electrons of N promote the chelation of –NH2 and –COOH groups on the CDs surface, easily bounding to functional ligands (like organic molecules, polymers, proteins, and DNA) through amidation coordination, and electrostatic interactions, inducing upward shift of Fermi level and conduction band electrons [28]. This property minimizes the gap between HOMO and LUMO, requiring less energy for excitation, which can enhance the sensing performance of CDs. To the best of our knowledge, there are limited CDs-based sensors and no NCDs for detecting gefitinib. Hence, there is still interest in developing a NCDs-based sensor for rapid and sensitive determination of gefitinib.

Various quenching mechanisms of NCDs, including energy transfer, photoinduced electron transfer, static and dynamic quenching, and inner filter effect (IFE), have been identified in the literature [29]. Generally, IFE refers to the phenomenon that the absorption bands of the absorber in the reaction system may overlap with the excitation or emission spectrum of the fluorophore, which leads to the fluorescence quenching of the fluorophore [30]. Compared with other fluorescence sensing mechanisms, IFE does not require complex surface functionalized fluorescent materials or covalent coupling between receptors and fluorophores. Therefore, IFE has shown greater advantages in developing more sensitive and straightforward fluorescence sensors.

In this work, the NCDs based on IFE were designed as the fluorescence sensor for the determination of gefitinib (Scheme 1 ). The NCDs were prepared through simple one-pot hydrothermal route using p-phenylenediamine (PPD) and D-glutamic acid (D-Glu) as the precursors. The obtained NCDs exhibited blue fluorescence and specific recognition of gefitinib. After interacting with gefitinib, the emission peak at 345 nm displayed obvious fluorescence quenching because of the IFE mechanism. The developed probe has been proven to have excellent sensing properties for gefitinib with quick response, high sensitivity, and good recyclability.

D-glutamic acid (D-Glu), p-phenylenediamine (PPD), glutathione (GSH), glucose (Glu), cysteine (Gys), tryptophan (Trp), valine (Val), labetalol, ofloxacin, and norfloxacin were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). CaCl2, MgSO4, FeCl3, and ZnSO4 were brought from Chron Chemicals Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). The chemicals employed in this paper were of analytical grade without further purification.

The Cary 60 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA) and Edinburgh FS5 spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh, UK) were used to collect the spectral data, which included UV–vis absorption and fluorescence (FL) spectra. The Nicolet IS50 fourier transform-infrared spectroscopy (FT–IR) spectrometer (Thermo, USA) was applied to measure the FT–IR data. The FEI Tecnai G2 F20 TEM equipment (FEI, USA) was utilized to scan the data for the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image. The K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrometer (Thermo, USA) was utilized to retrieve the XPS data. Zeta potential assessment was performed through a Zetasizer nano ZS90 (Malvern, UK).

The preparation of NCDs was mainly based on our previous work and slightly modified [31]. The NCDs were fabricated by one-pot hydrothermal process, with PPD and D-Glu as raw materials. Briefly, 0.1 g of PPD and 0.1 g of D-Glu were formulated with 20 mL of H2O/EtOH (V/V, 1:1). Subsequently, the mixed solution was transferred into a 30 mL of stainless-steel autoclave lined with Teflon, followed by heating at 180 °C for 12 h. The crude product was centrifuged to discard the precipitate (12000 rpm, 10 min). The collected supernatant was purified by dialysis membrane (MWCO: 1000 Da) for 8 h. The NCDs powder was obtained through freeze drying, followed by storage in a desiccator.

For the detection of gefitinib, 1 mL of NCDs solution (40 μg/mL) was mixed with different volumes of gefitinib work solution (300 μg/mL) or sample solutions, and then diluted with distilled water to 2 mL. The final concentration of gefitinib ranged from 0 to 30 μg/mL. After incubation at room temperature for 5 min, the FL spectra were measured under 240 nm excitation wavelength. The FL intensity at 345 nm was utilized for the quantitative evaluation of gefitinib. The selectivity of gefitinib was further validated through a similar approach when exposed to interfering substances or other drugs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of gefitinib in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the practical application capability, the sensing performance of fluorescent probe on human serum was further studied. Human blood samples were collected from local volunteers at the First Affiliated Hospital of Chongqing Medical University. The plus-target recovery method was utilized to evaluate the accuracy of the method, which was designed at three concentration points of 5, 15, and 30 µg/mL. The fluorescence detection process was the same as the above steps, and all fluorescence tests were conducted at least 3 times.

The morphological structure and size distribution of NCDs were determined by TEM. It was found that the NCDs displayed nearly spherical with good dispersion and uniformity (Fig. 1 A). The size of NCDs distributed within the range of 1.2–5.2 nm, with an average value of 2.8 nm. The UV–vis absorption and FL spectra were utilized to comprehensively study the optical characteristics of NCDs. The UV–vis spectrum of NCDs exhibited two absorption bands centered at 238 and 285 nm (Fig. 1B), which were attributed to the π-π* transition of aromatic rings and the n–π* transition of the C = O bond, respectively [32]. Meanwhile, the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths could be determined as 240 and 345 nm, respectively (Fig. 1B). The FL spectra revealed that the FL emissions from NCDs were consistent regardless of the excitation wavelength, and all emission peaks occurred at 345 nm, indicating that that the particle size of NCDs is extremely uniform (Fig. 1C) [33,34]. Furthermore, the zeta potentials of NCDs, gefitinib, and NCDs-gefitinib were 22.83 ± 1.91, −0.64 ± 0.50, and 22.27 ± 3.09 mV, respectively (Fig. S1). After the introduction of gefitinib, the positive potential of NCDs slightly decreased due to the negative potential of gefitinib. These results indicate the existence of electrostatic interaction between NCDs and gefitinib.

In order to determine the chemical functional groups in the NCDs, the FT–IR analysis was performed. As shown in Fig. 1D, the broader absorption peak in the region of 3100–3600 cm−1 is attributed to the O–H/N–H stretching vibrations [35]. The sharp peaks between 1610 and 1675 cm−1 derive from the stretching vibration of C = O and C = C/C = N [36]. The low-intensity bonds around 2928 cm−1 can be identified as the stretching vibrations of C–H. The peaks at 1495 and 1345 cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of C–N and C–O, respectively [37]. The XPS measurements were conducted to identify the surface elemental composition and chemical states of NCDs (Fig. 2 A). It was observed that the NCDs comprised three elements: C, O, and N, respectively, with concentrations of 59.81 %, 12.95 %, and 10.82 %. The three peaks at 285.98, 287.98, and 284.88 eV in the high-resolution spectrum of C 1s were assigned to the C–O, C = O, and C = C/C–C bonds, respectively (Fig. 2B) [36,38]. The spectrum of O 1s showed two peaks at 530.98 and 531.98 eV, corresponding to the C = O, C–O bonds (Fig. 2C). For the N 1s spectrum, two peaks at 399.48 and 401.48 eV were attributed to C–N–C and N–H bonds, respectively (Fig. 2D). The XPS analysis and FT–IR data are in good agreement, revealing the surface of NCDs is rich in carboxyl, amino, and hydroxyl groups contributing to strong water solubility.

When the as-prepared NCDs were tested in addition to gefitinib, significant fluorescence quenching was observed (Fig. S2). In order to achieve high sensitivity and a wide linear range of the sensor, various sensing conditions were optimized. Firstly, different concentrations of NCDs were examined (Fig. S3A). When the concentration of NCDs increased from 20 to 120 μg/mL, the fluorescence intensity (F0) elevated initially, then gradually dropped, and reached its peak value at 40 μg/mL. Meanwhile, the quenching efficiency ((F0-F)/F0) remained essentially unchanged. Therefore, we determined that the ideal concentration of NCDs for better sensitivity and broader response range is 40 μg/mL. As shown in Fig. S4B, the F0 achieved its maximum value at pH 4, while the (F0-F)/F0 at pH 6, indicating that the sensor performed better in slightly acidic environments, which may be attributed to protonation effects [39]. The response time is a crucial factor that affects the practicability of the probe. As presented in Fig. S4C, after introducing gefitinib, both F0 and (F0-F)/F0 remained nearly constant from 0 to 70 min, highlighting the rapidity and convenience of NCDs as a fluorescent probe for detecting gefitinib. As depicted in Fig. S4D, the F0 and (F0-F)/F0 of the sensor changed minimally with reaction temperatures ranging from 0 to 70 °C, indicating the stability of NCDs and the sensor's ability to detect gefitinib at room temperature.

Calibration curve Under the above optimal sensing conditions, we investigated the analytical performances of the probe. When the concentration of gefitinib gradually increased (0–30 μg/mL), the fluorescence intensity progressively weakened (Fig. 3 A). As illustrated in Fig. 3B, there was a good linear relationship between the logarithmic value of F/F0 and the concentration of gefitinib ranging from 0.025 to 30 μg/mL, lg(F/F0) = −0.04899C + 0.00448 (R2 = 0.9997). The limit of detection (LOD) of gefitinib was determined as follows: LOD = 3δ/k, resulting in a value of 5.5 ng/mL [40]. As shown in Table 1 , compared with other analytical techniques reported previously, the NCDs-based nanoprobe displayed a broader linear range and lower detection limit toward gefitinib. The CIE chromaticity diagram showed that the color of the NCDs solution became darker as the concentration of gefitinib increased (Fig. S4). These results demonstrated the NCDs-based probe for detecting gefitinib owns overwhelmingly feasible merits like simplicity and economy.

Interference of foreign substances In the practical application, the selectivity and anti-interference properties of the probe play critical roles. As seen from Fig. 4 , various possible interfering substances including metal ions, amino acids, and other some drugs were chosen to study the specificity of fluorescent probe. The highest quenching effect was observed upon the addition of gefitinib, while other interfering substances did not have a notable influence on the FL intensity of NCDs. These results showed that the NCDs have good selectivity for gefitinib.

Real sample detection To further verify the practicality of the sensor, we selected human serum as the actual sample in this study, and the results were presented in Table 2 . The initial concentration in real samples was 0 μg/mL, and we spiked them with amounts of 5, 15, and 30 μg/mL of gefitinib. The recoveries of the spiked samples fell within the range of 98.98 %–110.60 %, with RSD of ≤ 3.98 %. These results affirm the accuracy of the sensor for detecting gefitinib in real samples.

The mechanisms underlying the fluorescence responses of NCDs to gefitinib were thoroughly investigated. As displayed in Fig. 5 A, when gefitinib was introduced to the NCDs, there was no alteration in the existing UV–vis absorption peaks, and no new peaks emerged. This indicated that the static quenching, as described in reference [29,43], did not occur. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of gefitinib overlapped with the FL excitation spectrum of NCDs (Fig. 5B). This observation led to the hypothesis that the fluorescence quenching mechanism might be associated with the IFE [44]. Additionally, the fluorescence lifetimes of NCDs remained nearly constant, with values of 1.64 and 1.67 ns before and after the introduction of gefitinib, respectively (Fig. 5C). These suggested that dynamic quenching and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) did not play a significant role [45]. Moreover, it was worth noting that the FT–IR spectrum of NCDs, when combined with gefitinib (Fig. 5D), did not reveal any significant new functional groups. This finding demonstrated that the NCDs remain sufficiently stable in water, thereby excluding the possibility of fluorescence quenching due to structural damage. In summary, we concluded that the primary mechanism by which gefitinib quenches the fluorescence of NCDs is through the IFE.

In conclusion, the NCDs were fabricated through a simple, economical and eco-friendly hydrothermal method, utilizing PPD and D-Glu as starting materials. The obtained NCDs were monodispersed and uniform spherical morphology, and displayed outstanding optical performances. It was found that gefitinib can quickly quench the fluorescence of NCDs through an IFE mechanism between NCDs and gefitinib. Leveraging these attributes, we designed an efficient NCDs-based fluorescent probe for rapid and ultrasensitive detection of gefitinib. The sensor displayed a broad linear range, high sensitivity, and exceptional selectivity. Importantly, we successfully employed the nanoprobe to monitor gefitinib in human serum samples. This method stands out for its simplicity, convenience, affordability, and effectiveness, making it highly promising for potential applications in clinical therapeutic drug monitoring.

Yuxin He: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Validation, Writing – original draft. Hanting Wang: Formal analysis, Investigation, Software, Writing – original draft. Yuting Yan: Investigation, Software. Xinhui Jiang: Supervision, Validation. Hecun Zou: Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Zhengwei Zhang: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (82003713 and 82003854), the Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (cstc2020jcyj-msxmX0158 and cstc2021jcyj-msxmX0302), and CQMU Program for Youth Innovation in Future Medicine (W0152).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.123942.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
